---
title: "Lab 1"
author: "Nathan Alexander"
date: "2024-09-11"
output:
  html_document:
    theme: flatly
  pdf_document: default
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: sentence
---

```{r, eval=F}
install.packages("devtools")
library(devtools)
```

You can write you own syntax, tell me how your day is going, or what you plan to do in this lab.

```{r, eval=F}
# install package
install.packages("tidyverse", repos = "http://cran.us.r-project.org")
install.packages("remotes", repos = "http://cran.us.r-project.org")
 
# load the necessary libraries
library(tidyverse) #collection of R packages designed for data science
library(here) #helps with filepaths
library(dplyr)
library(remotes)

here::i_am("lab-template.Rmd")
```

Type a note.



```{r, eval=F}


```


```{r, eval=F}


```






















# Preparing a lab report

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
# install.packages("reticulate",repos = "http://cran.us.r-project.org")
install.packages("educationdata",repos = "http://cran.us.r-project.org")
library(reticulate)
library(educationdata)
```

## Using R Markdown

In RStudio, open an R Markdown document by selecting File \> New File \> R Markdown.
When the dialog box opens, you can add a preliminary name to the file (but this is not yet required until you save the file), so you can simply click 'OK'.
At the top of the R Markdown document, enter the following code (include the three dashes at the top and bottom):

```         

```

This is an R Markdown document.
Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents.
For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document.
You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

```{python}
x = [1, 2, 3]
```

```{r}
py$x
```

```{r}
py$y <- head(cars)
```

```{python}
print(y)
```

# Using different code {.tabset}

## R

(tab content)

## Python

(tab content)
